sentences.text: No.
Nothing that happened during that time.
Only when we started organizing for the second ship, which was sometime in between.
We had trouble from the Jewish establishment.
They said, it's perilous, irresponsible.
These people can be caught.
All kinds of things can happen to them.
But it was only in the verbal stage.
They questioned me.
They called me.
I was the one who was the Secretary of State for the-- because I was a lawyer.
The others, [INAUDIBLE] was a very brilliant man, but he was the other, unknown journalist.
And the others were businessmen, and not known in the public.
But my name was already known in the public, too, in the Jewish public, because I was supposed to be a good speaker, and I'd spoken at many meetings.
Jewish Zionist meetings.
That things are easy.
Yeah.
The second ship there were difficulties with the Jewish community.
They tried to find out whom we have registered with anybody from Vienna among them.
But we kept it secret.
The second ship, there were all the trouble for the second ship, we had the cooperation of the police.
We needed it, because the people came in without any means.
And we had to get them together to one place.
We had to prepare them for the trip, too.
And we rented-- the Betar did this.
Otto Seidmann did it.
Got the permission for to [INAUDIBLE] to collect these people in an abandoned castle, Kottingbrunn, K-O-T-T-I-N-G-B-R-U-N-N, about 10 or 15 miles from Vienna.
And we had some trouble here with the police, because the people came in, and they were hanging around in Vienna, some of them before we had this Kottingbrunn ready.
And we said they should be as inconspicuous as possible.
They should wear shorts, shorts, because young people of this age usually wear short pants.
Though they wore pants which they had cut off, here and here, and you could see that there was something wrong with it.
Though we discussed it with the police, that we are trying to get Jews who are otherwise a burden to Vienna, because they are here without means, but we are trying to get them out.
And they fully cooperated with us.
We had cooperated with the police before this already.
I was quite active [INAUDIBLE] to tell the police that we organized a boycott of German goods.
The police knew about it.
And we achieved, for example, from the Ministry of Transportation, the trains which usually went to Paris and London via-- via Germany, because it's the shortest way.
But some of these trains were diverted and went via Switzerland, avoiding Germany.
We had contact with the police.
Which, there was a man called Ulbricht there whose name I remember-- U-L-B-R-I-C-H-T.
And there was a Jewish high officer, Gans, G-A-N-S.
High police officer, from whom we got cooperation with getting the Jews who are unwanted, out of Austria.

But many-- at the end, most of the people came from the [INAUDIBLE], from Czechoslovakia, where the need-- in Poland, where the need was not so urgent as it was in the most eastern part of Czechoslovakia, in Podkarpacka Rus.
Mukachevo was the capital of this part of Czechoslovakia.
We collected the people in Kottingbrunn, as they came.
And the Betar in Vienna knew of it.
And very few of them still availed themselves of it.
But there were only Betar in from Vienna.
And I traveled to, I gave speeches in Slovakia, Northern Podkarpacka Rus.
I didn't go that far east anymore.
In Trnovo, I remember one.
Trnovo.
T-R-N-O-V-O.
[INAUDIBLE] to the young people.
And they came from there, so we had no problems with them.
As soon as they arrived, they were brought to Kottingbrunn, and there the Betar took over the training.
They were trained in a little bit of Hebrew.
They were trained in geography of Israel.
They were trained in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] close combat, close combat fighting, the first ones still.
Street fighting.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were trained in Zionist history, and in Jewish history and in Zionist history.
And Betar, this is a very good job in this.
Also in close-order drill.
[INAUDIBLE] did.
The money came, largely, from one rich Viennese, Mr.
Peruz, P-E-R-U-Z.
He was a dealer in cotton, and was a very rich man, and not much money was required at that time.
Transport was very cheap.
I think we paid about six pounds, which was $30 a person, for a ship.
And I don't remember anymore how much it was.
But Peruz give the money.
From the Zionist organization I didn't get any help.
The Betarim did.
Yes.
There was a member, a very leading, the leading, Zionist in Vienna was [GERMAN], that was his title, Robert Stricker-- S-T-R-I-C-K-E-R.
He had been a member of Parliament in the First Austrian Republic, after the Parliament broke down.
And he wasn't reelected, but he was the unspoken leader, and he was a speaker and very well-known, and he helped us in every way but he could.
He was a vice president of the Jewish community.
I think so.
Nobody, at that time.
Yes, I had-- we had opposition.
They tried to pursue that there were people who we know were [INAUDIBLE] in community who called the parents of the young people, don't let them go, this is an adventure.
Very young people do not know what they are doing.
And there was no real emergency in Austria then.
No.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] None of us did.
For the second and third ship, which were before Hitler arrived in Vienna, the money were provided by Peruz and some well-to-do friends of his.
For the next transport, which came already after Hitler, we had very much resistance from the community first.
When they persuaded-- they tried to persuade people not to come with us, and I tricked them, over-ranked them with the [INAUDIBLE].
We had already the passports of so and so many people.
And we told them, you can control us by giving you the passports.
And they were stupid enough to accept these passports.
And after a few days, we started a rumor that a transport is leaving within a very short time, and we need everybody's passports.
Throughout the community, go and get it.
And [INAUDIBLE] and there were lines standing there, and there were shouts and shoving.
They wanted their passports.
So the important thing for us was we got-- they gave the passports to us, not to the boy.
That this will become known, that the community, somehow, unofficially, cooperates with us.
Yeah.
Well, the evening the Anschluss occurred, the Austrian government fell, is, of course, very lively in my memory.
It was a Friday evening.
And I was for shabbat been invited by a friend of mine, Dr.
David Buckspan, B-U-C-K-S-P-A-N, and his parents.
And we, just as the meal was served, the Austrian Chancellor, it was announced that the Austrian Chancellor will give an important, make an important announcement.
A few minutes afterwards, he announced that he had been forced to resign.
And to avoid blood, under the force, threatened with the force of the German army, and under the threat, under this threat, he is resigning.
And his last words were 'Gott schutze Osterreich,' God protect Austria.
What?
Immediately, the streets were crowded with rowdies, and with the Nazis.
And Nazis was, until then, been hidden.
You know, it was, Nazis was forbidden in Austria.
They all came out of their hidden places.
And they wore uniforms.
But very few wore uniform at this time.
But many Viennese joined in.
There was just noise in the streets.
And I don't think any Jews-- maybe some Jews were beaten up in the sane night.
It's very likely.
I had an interesting experience.
I, at one corner, I saw a group of people standing around a woman, and I went there.
And there was a prostitute there, telling the people that it's wrong what you are doing, and what you want to do to the Jews.
The Jews are just as good people as anybody else.
Nobody spoke up for the Jews, but she spoke up.
Horror.
Horror.
And they run to the consulates.
Many tried to flee to the nearby borders.
The Czechoslovakian border was only about 40 or 60 miles away.
The Hungarian border was very close.
They tried to escape by taking the next train to Bohemia, to Czechoslovakia, or to Hungary.
But these trains are all stopped on the border by the-- by the SR.
It was not the SS yet.
It was the SR.
Until the people there returned, their names were taken because they tried to escape, and so they returned to Vienna.
But some very smart people took-- did not try to go to this near border, but they took-- traveled to Italy, where there was no problem yet of refugees on the border.
And they traveled to Germany, to France, and there was nothing yet in the passports against the Jews.
There was no law yet that they couldn't go.
And they crossed into France with a very good neutral Austrian passport.
Dr.
Wolfgang [INAUDIBLE] was one of them.
And Dr.
Chester, ?] a leading member of the Revisionist Party, Edmund [?
Chester, ?] he lives here, now, in Washington.
He escaped the day after that to Italy.
I don't know how opposed they were.
I think they-- well, after this experience with the passport, they officially, no, unofficially surrendered to our action.
We got money from them by the help of Stricker and by the help of the leader of the Assimilationists, Dr.
Josef Ticho, T-I-C-H-O.
He was the leader of the Assimilationists, and he was the Vice President of the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], Jewish community, and by his help, too, we got money for the transport.
[INAUDIBLE] was our treasurer, and she took care of all this.
My work as a person?
As a lawyer?
I didn't work with the Jewish community.
No, getting the Jews out is what I mean.
I'm sorry.
Well, we met and planned how we could organize the next transport.
And it was decided, it's not possible now.
We have to see how the-- we have to decide to get the permission from the Gestapo.
Otherwise, everybody will be arrested, and the community will be right in saying that we-- the idea was not to move, not to show.
So we didn't do anything in terms of transports.
But we did do one thing.
We took the lists of those who would be ready to go, of these young people, and told them, get a passport.
To get the passport, you have to have a-- young people usually didn't have passports.
But they checked whether you didn't owe any taxes.
There was some delay before you get your passport.
And this way, they got their passports, and they brought the passports to us.
From 9 or 10 years, to 22 or 25 years.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] I would say 22 years about. 25 years too high.
I approached them in a rather dramatic interview with Eichmann.

After a few weeks of the German presence in Vienna, where everything was in the open, nobody knows what is going to happen, everybody run to the consulates to get visa, and no consulates gives you entrance visas, they all close their doors to the threatened inflow of refugees, probably without money.
Just people without money coming into the country.
And one day-- the day should be establishable from the Jewish history of Vienna.

One day, Eichmann, who was in charge of Jewish problems for Vienna, orders all the leaders of the Jewish organizations to come to the office of the Zionist organization, some office of the Zionist organization.
And, you know, in Marc-Aurel-Strasse-- it was, I believe, Marc, M-A-R-C, Aurel, A-U-R-E-L, Strasse, he was a Roman emperor who founded Vienna.
And from every group, the people came, as ordered.
They were not ordered by names, but it was became known very fast.
Of the Revisionists, most of them weren't here anymore.
And I went for the Revisionists.
I was not, at that time, a member of the Revisionist, of the Revisionist leadership.
I don't think so.
But I went, anyhow, and Turi was here.
Turi was never a member of the Viennese organization, of the leadership.
Turi and I went for the Revisionist organization to this meeting with Eichmann.
And after [INAUDIBLE] people, then went out of the 10 or 15 minutes, sometimes shorter, sometimes longer, we were called in.
Revisionist organization.
And we came in, and Eichmann asked me first for our name, our addresses, and then somebody took for him.
And he then asked me, what, where is now a certain name, which I do not recall.
I think I mention it in the book, in then I still know it.
I did not know this name.
But I found out later, this was a man who has organized the anti-German boycott in the Baltic countries.
And they were very much after this guy.
He had organized the boycott [INAUDIBLE].
The man was very organized at that time yet.
And I told him, I do not know.
And he put me, said, turn around, and put me, and I stood with my face towards the wall with my back to him, and he pointed his gun at me.
Not he pointed, I felt the pressure of the gun in my back.
And he said, I want to know, and you will tell me now, where is he, whatever name is.
And if you don't tell me, I will give you a second belly button, or another asshole.
And I was paralyzed.

But after I call out as he counted and say after two, or maybe even before two, but before three, I told him, I have some information which you very much would like to have, and which is much more important, probably, than the name of this man.
So he said, tell me your swindles, your swindling scheme.
And I told him that we had organized transports to Palestine.
And he said, put it down in writing.
And then after, a short time later, he knew that we had organized boycotts in Germany, apparently, too.
He took [INAUDIBLE] and threw it out, and me.
I don't know whether there was somebody else in this police vehicle where you transport arrested people to the office of the Revisionist organization.
There, we had loads of correspondence with, regarding, the boycott of German goods.
But Turi [?
Deutsche ?] has put them all in a heap in front of the door, on the side, on the wall.
And it's unbelievable.
They searched the office and didn't look at this bunch there in front of the wall.
They searched the office only very superficially.
And then they sent us home.
And I then composed the very thoughtful article, the memorandum, which I give gave to Eichmann, which I have very recently made a translation because the original is hardly readable.
Original was with The Nazis, but I had carbon copies of it.
And the carbon copies, in the course of 50 years, you didn't see too much of it.
But I had a carbon copy.
I gave one carbon copy, I believe, to the museum, to the Holocaust Museum.
I don't know.
But I had the carbon copy, which I could make out, because I knew what it was.
And I have it.
I can read it to you, the memo now.
In which I made it clear to him that we have the same interest as they have, to move the Jews out, and that we have the means to move them out.
And I asked him just not to interfere.
First of all, he got this only after this meeting.
When I saw him the next time, when I entered the-- when I was waiting to see Robert Stricker in the [INAUDIBLE] of the Jewish community, I was sitting there.
I don't know whether I alone or with somebody else.
I assume with somebody else from our organization.
We never were alone.
Eichmann with his helper, [INAUDIBLE], came by.
And when he saw me there, he stopped and said, [GERMAN].
This, translated into English, with these transports, [GERMAN].
This is out.
These transports are out.
We do not need a center for criminals in Palestine.
The Jews will be atomized.
At this time, 'atomized' had another meaning than today.
It meant [? distributed ?] and broken into many pieces all over the world.
Yes.
Yes.
I went not around.
I went on top of him.
I went with Silberschein to Berlin.
Silberschein has the little flag, a British flag, because he was a Palestinian citizen.
And I had nothing, but we had tickets to Berlin.
And the SR, who controlled the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] passengers, asked Silberschein.
And he said he comes with me for an important mission in Berlin.
So we were going.
We went to the man who was in charge of the Revisionist organization in Berlin, Dr.
Kraski, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] K-R-A-S-K-I.
And we told him we have to go to Eichmann's superiors, and to complain about him.
This is a very risky things, but we have to, because he asked-- not because he asked the [INAUDIBLE] against his own interests, against the interests of the German Reich.
He doesn't understand it.
And [INAUDIBLE] understand it you'll tell this man.
He said, you are crazy.
You cannot complain about a German official to his superior.
But what you can do is, you go, you need foreign currency.
You go to the Ministry of Foreign-- to the Treasury.
And try to get foreign currency from them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we-- I-- I went to a [GERMAN], an office where the secretaries type, because outside the building, a ministerium, and dictated there.
I have the copy of what we had dictated to Eichmann, with a copy which I'll give you, and added to it that we need, for this purpose, money.
Yeah.
